name: The Ballcourt of the Gods: Stories from the Maya
description:
K'iche' Maya mythology in rhyming verse for bedtime. The silence before creation, the false sun's vanity, the first ballplayers who descended to the underworld, and the Hero Twins who defeated the lords of death not with weapons but with TRICKS — and then became the sun and the moon so that humans could be made from CORN.

The Popol Vuh is a survival document. When Spanish colonizers burned Maya books in the 1500s, anonymous K'iche' scribes wrote down their sacred stories in Latin script to preserve them. Every story in this grammar exists because someone refused to let their world be erased. The Hero Twins — Hunahpu and Xbalanque — are perhaps the most creative heroes in all world mythology: they defeat death by dying on purpose, coming back as catfish, then as wandering performers, tricking the lords of Xibalba into asking to be killed. No swords. No armies. Just cleverness, sacrifice, and corn.

Source: Dennis Tedlock translation (1996), Allen J. Christenson translation (2007). Based on the K'iche' Maya manuscript preserved in the Newberry Library, Chicago.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Maya codex pages — the Dresden Codex (c. 13th century, celestial tables and deity figures), Madrid Codex (rituals and daily life), Paris Codex (zodiacal pages). Frederick Catherwood (1799-1854) — extraordinary detailed lithographs of Maya ruins from Incidents of Travel in Central America, Chiapas, and Yucatan (1841) with John Lloyd Stephens. Maya ceramic rollout photographs — painted vessels depicting the Hero Twins, ballgame scenes, and Xibalba lords. Diego Rivera (1886-1957) — murals in the Palacio Nacional depicting pre-Columbian Mesoamerican civilization (note: Rivera's work enters public domain 70 years after death, check jurisdiction).

sections.Story:
Before the WORLD, before the LIGHT,
Before the stars came out at NIGHT,
There was no EARTH, there was no SKY,
Just SILENCE stretching, still and HIGH.

No BIRD to sing, no WIND to blow,
No RIVER running far BELOW,
No MOUNTAIN rising from the GROUND —
Just STILLNESS. Not a single SOUND.

The SEA lay flat. The SKY was bare.
Only WATER everywhere.
And hidden in that quiet DEEP,
The GODS were thinking in their SLEEP.
sections.Wonder: Before the world, there was only stillness and water — no sounds at all. If you were one of the sleeping gods, what is the FIRST sound you would want to create?
sections.Whisper: The Popol Vuh opens with one of the most beautiful creation scenes in world literature: 'There is not yet one person, one animal, bird, fish, crab, tree, rock, hollow, canyon, meadow, forest. Only the sky alone is there; the face of the earth is not clear.' The K'iche' word for this primordial state suggests not emptiness but POTENTIAL — everything waiting to unfold. Compare with the Egyptian Nun and the Hebrew tehom.
sections.Original:
— Lewis Spence, Popol Vuh: Mythic and Heroic Sagas of the Kichés —

It is worthy of notice that the Kiché myth embodies the general aboriginal idea of creation which prevailed in the New World. In many of them the central idea of creation is supplied by the brooding of a great bird over the dark primeval waste of waters. Thus the Athapascans thought that a mighty raven, with eyes of fire and wings whose clapping was as the thunder, descended to the ocean and raised the earth to its surface. [7] The Muscokis believed that a couple of pigeons, skimming the surface of the deep, espied a blade of grass upon its surface, which slowly evolved into the dry land. [8] The Zuñis imagined that Awonawilona, the All-father, so impregnated the waters that a scum appeared upon their surface which became the earth and sky. [9] The Iroquois said that their female ancestor, expelled from heaven by her angry spouse, landed upon the sea, from which mud at once arose. The Mixtecs imagined that two winds--those of the Nine Serpents and the Nine Caverns--under the guise of a bird and a winged serpent respectively, caused the waters to subside and the land to appear.

metadata.maya_source: Popol Vuh, Preamble — the description of the primordial sea before creation. Tedlock translation pp. 63-64
metadata.archetype: The primordial void — the formless potential before creation (parallels Egyptian Nun, Norse Ginnungagap, Hindu unmanifest Brahman)
metadata.pop_culture: Like the opening of Coco — before the music starts, before the marigold petals fall, there's just darkness and a voice about to tell you everything

sections.Story:
Deep in the WATER, green and BRIGHT,
A SERPENT glowed with feathered LIGHT.
His name was Q'UKUMATZ — the PLUMED,
With emerald SCALES, in quetzal PLUMED.

He floated there with HURRICANE —
The Heart of Sky, who BRINGS the rain.
Two GODS together, still as STONE,
Waiting in the dark ALONE.

They THOUGHT. They DREAMED. They planned it RIGHT.
The serpent in the WATER bright,
The hurricane up in the SKY —
Both WONDERING: what if we TRY?
sections.Wonder: The Plumed Serpent waited in the water and the Heart of Sky waited above — they were both alone until they found each other. Have you ever had a friend who helped you be brave enough to try something new?
sections.Whisper: Q'ukumatz (K'iche' for 'Quetzal Serpent') is the same deity as Aztec Quetzalcoatl and Yucatec Kukulkan — the feathered serpent who bridges earth and sky. In the Popol Vuh, creation requires PARTNERSHIP: Q'ukumatz (water/earth) and Juraqan/Hurricane (sky/wind) must think TOGETHER. No single god creates alone. The word 'hurricane' in English comes directly from Juraqan — the Maya god's name became our word for the most powerful storms.
sections.Original:
— Lewis Spence, Popol Vuh: Mythic and Heroic Sagas of the Kichés —

That Quetzalcohuatl was not an aboriginal Maya-Kiché deity is proved by the relative importance granted him by a people--the Aztecâ--to whom he was alien; and that they regarded him as the aboriginal god of Anahuac par excellence is indisputable.

Hurakan, the winged creative power, is the wind of the tempest. [12] In the "Popol Vuh" he is designated "The Heart of Heaven." He is parallel with if not identical to the Aztecan deity Tezcatlipoca, who in his variant of Yoalli-ehecatl (the Wind of Night) was supplicated by the Aztecâ as the life-breath. [13] Elsewhere we have hinted that Tezcatlipoca may have been an ice-god. [14] Mr. Payne sees in him an elaboration of the vision of death in a polished "scrying"-stone, which seems possible but scarcely probable. Hurakan was in all likelihood derived from an original deity of the Antilles. [15] The term "hurricane" is said to have originated from the name of this god, and although the direct evidence for this is scanty, other circumstances place the connection beyond reasonable doubt. Hurakan is also alluded to in the "Popol Vuh," as "The Strong Serpent," and "He who hurls below," referring to his presence in the lightning.

metadata.maya_source: Popol Vuh, Part 1 — Q'ukumatz and Juraqan (Heart of Sky) as co-creators. Christenson translation pp. 58-60
metadata.archetype: The creator pair — two complementary forces that must unite to create (parallels Chinese yin-yang, Hindu Shiva-Shakti, Polynesian Rangi-Papa)
metadata.pop_culture: Like Miguel and Héctor in Coco — neither can cross between worlds alone, but together they can do the impossible

sections.Story:
'Let it be DONE!' the two gods CRIED.
'Let the WATER pull aside!
Let the EARTH come rising UP!
Let the world fill like a CUP!'

And when they SPOKE, the mountains GREW.
The RIVERS found their paths and FLEW.
The VALLEYS opened, wide and GREEN.
The most amazing thing you've SEEN.

The WORD made everything appear —
A FOREST there, a CANYON here.
The gods said 'EARTH!' and earth OBEYED.
That's how the whole wide WORLD was made.
sections.Wonder: The gods didn't BUILD the world with their hands — they SPOKE it into being. Words have real power! What is one word you would speak if it could become something real?
sections.Whisper: In the Popol Vuh, creation happens through SPEECH: 'Earth!' they said, and it was instantly made. This parallels the Hebrew Genesis ('Let there be light') and the Egyptian Hu (divine utterance), but with a key difference — the Maya creators must speak TOGETHER. The K'iche' word 'tz'aqol' (creator) literally means 'builder' or 'maker,' suggesting creation is both a craft and an utterance. Words in Maya thought are not descriptions of reality; they are the TECHNOLOGY of reality.

— Lewis Spence, Popol Vuh: Mythic and Heroic Sagas of the Kichés —

It is worthy of notice that the Kiché myth embodies the general aboriginal idea of creation which prevailed in the New World. In many of them the central idea of creation is supplied by the brooding of a great bird over the dark primeval waste of waters. Thus the Athapascans thought that a mighty raven, with eyes of fire and wings whose clapping was as the thunder, descended to the ocean and raised the earth to its surface. [7] The Muscokis believed that a couple of pigeons, skimming the surface of the deep, espied a blade of grass upon its surface, which slowly evolved into the dry land. [8] The Zuñis imagined that Awonawilona, the All-father, so impregnated the waters that a scum appeared upon their surface which became the earth and sky. [9] The Iroquois said that their female ancestor, expelled from heaven by her angry spouse, landed upon the sea, from which mud at once arose. The Mixtecs imagined that two winds--those of the Nine Serpents and the Nine Caverns--under the guise of a bird and a winged serpent respectively, caused the waters to subside and the land to appear.

metadata.maya_source: Popol Vuh, Part 1 — the creation of the earth through divine speech. 'Earth!' they said, and instantly it was made. Tedlock pp. 64-65
metadata.archetype: Creation through utterance — the divine word that makes reality (parallels Hebrew 'Let there be light,' Hindu Om, Egyptian Hu)
metadata.pop_culture: Like Moana when the ocean first responds to her voice — speaking to the world and having it LISTEN

sections.Story:
The gods tried FIRST to make a RACE
Of people with a HUMAN face.
They scooped up MUD from river BANKS
And shaped a body — no one THANKS.

The mud-folk CRUMBLED in the RAIN.
Their words were MUSH, they had no BRAIN.
They couldn't TURN their heads to PRAY,
And when the water came — they WASHED away.

'That DIDN'T work,' the makers SAID.
'They can't even hold up their own HEAD.
We need a BETTER kind of stuff
To make a person STRONG enough.'
sections.Wonder: The gods' FIRST try at making people was mud — and they fell apart in the rain! Have you ever made something that didn't work the first time? The gods weren't embarrassed — they just tried again. What would YOU try again?
sections.Whisper: The mud people represent the first of THREE failed attempts at creating humanity — a pattern unique to Mesoamerican mythology. Most traditions get it right on the first try (or the second). The Maya gods need FOUR attempts, and each failure teaches them something. The mud people can speak but their words are meaningless; they have FORM without CONSCIOUSNESS. The Popol Vuh treats creation as an iterative DESIGN process, not a single divine decree.
sections.Original:
— Lewis Spence, Popol Vuh: Mythic and Heroic Sagas of the Kichés —

It is evident that the divine brothers feared ridicule, and profiting from the disasters of their father and uncle made sure of knowing the names of the chief Xibalbans ere they set out. In like manner they avoided making an obeisance to the dummy figures to which their predecessors had bowed so profoundly. The American savage, grave and reserved, cannot abide ridicule. He shrinks from it in a manner which a less self-regarding or a more self-assured people cannot comprehend. The other tests--the "House of Tigers," and the "House of Cold," and the various torments mentioned in the Second Book are much what might be expected from a barbarian idea of death--no more horrible, perhaps, than the European idea of Hell in the Middle Ages, certainly not more fear-compelling than the picture of Dante.

The American peoples are at one in their belief in a Paradise, a Place of Joy, if not of Reward. Their Hades appears to have been reserved almost entirely for the unillustrious.

metadata.maya_source: Popol Vuh, Part 1 — the first creation of humans from mud. Christenson pp. 68-70
metadata.archetype: The failed creation — divine trial and error (parallels Greek Prometheus's attempts, Norse Ask and Embla, but unique in requiring multiple failures)
metadata.pop_culture: Like in The Road to El Dorado when Tulio and Miguel's first plan falls apart — sometimes the best adventures start with a spectacular failure

sections.Story:
Next the gods carved folk from WOOD —
They WALKED and TALKED, which felt so GOOD!
They built their HOMES, they had their KIDS,
They did exactly what the carving DID.

But something MISSING. Something WRONG.
Their hearts were HOLLOW all day LONG.
They couldn't FEEL. They couldn't LOVE.
They never looked up at the stars ABOVE.

Wood people WORKED but never CARED.
They ATE their food but never SHARED.
They used their TOOLS but never PRAYED.
A world of robots, wooden-MADE.
sections.Wonder: The wood people could walk and talk and build houses — but they couldn't FEEL anything. They never said thank you, never looked at the stars. What do you think is the difference between being ALIVE and just being able to MOVE?
sections.Whisper: The wood people are the Popol Vuh's most haunting creation: fully functional humans without CONSCIOUSNESS. They reproduce, build homes, and populate the earth, but they have no 'heart' (k'ux) — the Maya concept of spiritual awareness. This is not a story about physical failure but about the horror of a world full of beings who work perfectly but feel NOTHING. Some scholars read this as a critique of mechanical existence without gratitude to the sacred.
sections.Original:
— Lewis Spence, Popol Vuh: Mythic and Heroic Sagas of the Kichés —

Mystery veils the commencement of the Second Book of the "Popol Vuh." The theme is the birth and family of Hun-Ahpu and Xbalanque, and the scribe intimates that only half is to be told concerning the history of their father. Xpiyacoc and Xmucane, the father and mother deities, had two sons, Hunhun-Ahpu and Vukub-Hunahpu, the first being, so far as can be gathered, a bi-sexual personage. He had by a wife, Xbakiyalo, two sons, Hunbatz and Hunchouen, men full of wisdom and artistic genius. All of them were addicted to the recreation of dicing and playing at ball, and a spectator of their pastimes was Voc, the messenger of Hurakan. Xbakiyalo having died, Hunhun-Ahpu and Vukub-Hunahpu, leaving the former's sons behind, played a game of ball which in its progress took them into the vicinity of the realm of Xibalba (the underworld). This reached the ears of the monarchs of that place, Hun-Came and Vukub-Came, who, after consulting their counsellors, challenged the strangers to a game of ball, with the object of defeating and disgracing them.

For this purpose they dispatched four messengers in the shape of owls.

metadata.maya_source: Popol Vuh, Part 1 — the second creation, wooden effigies. 'They did not have souls, nor minds... they did not remember the Heart of Sky.' Tedlock pp. 67-69
metadata.archetype: The soulless creation — beings that function without awareness (parallels golem legends, Frankenstein's creature, the Buddhist 'hungry ghosts')
metadata.pop_culture: Like the robots in WALL-E who do their jobs perfectly but have forgotten WHY — until one little robot remembers how to care

sections.Story:
The gods sent RAIN to wash them OUT.
A FLOOD of darkness, scream and SHOUT!
The wood-folk's COOKING POTS rose up:
'You BURNED us!' cried each angry CUP.

Their GRINDING stones began to SPEAK:
'You CRUSHED us every day this WEEK!'
Their DOGS growled: 'You never fed us RIGHT!'
Everything REBELLED that night.

The WOOD-FOLK ran but had no PLACE.
They tried to climb but fell from GRACE.
The few that lived became the MONKEYS —
Swinging through the trees like furry FLUNKIES.
sections.Wonder: When the wooden people were destroyed, their COOKING POTS and DOGS and GRINDING STONES all complained about how badly they'd been treated! Even their things were mad at them. Do you think we should be kind to the world around us — not just people, but animals, plants, even the things we use?
sections.Whisper: The rebellion of household objects is one of the most extraordinary passages in world mythology. The Popol Vuh lists: dogs, turkeys, grinding stones, cooking pots, plates, tortilla griddles — ALL rise against their masters. This is radical ANIMISM: every object has a spirit that remembers how it was treated. The wood people become monkeys (k'oy), which is why the K'iche' say monkeys look like failed humans. This is not evolution — it is DEVOLUTION, a warning about what happens when consciousness is absent.
sections.Original:
— Lewis Spence, Popol Vuh: Mythic and Heroic Sagas of the Kichés —

THE FIRST BOOK

Over a universe wrapped in the gloom of a dense and primeval night passed the god Hurakan, the mighty wind. He called out "earth," and the solid land appeared. The chief gods took counsel; they were Hurakan, Gucumatz, the serpent covered with green feathers, and Xpiyacoc and Xmucane, the mother and father gods. As the result of their deliberations animals were created. But as yet man was not. To supply the deficiency the divine beings resolved to create mannikins carved out of wood. But these soon incurred the displeasure of the gods, who, irritated by their lack of reverence, resolved to destroy them. Then by the will of Hurakan, the Heart of Heaven, the waters were swollen, and a great flood came upon the mannikins of wood. They were drowned and a thick resin fell from heaven. The bird Xecotcovach tore out their eyes; the bird Camulatz cut off their heads; the bird Cotzbalam devoured their flesh; the bird Tecumbalam broke their bones and sinews and ground them into powder. Because they had not thought on Hurakan, therefore the face of the earth grew dark, and a pouring rain commenced, raining by day and by night.

metadata.maya_source: Popol Vuh, Part 1 — the destruction of the wooden people and the rebellion of their possessions. Tedlock pp. 69-72
metadata.archetype: The great flood — divine reset of a failed world (parallels Noah, Gilgamesh, Hindu Manu, Greek Deucalion) — but uniquely includes rebellion of objects
metadata.pop_culture: Like Toy Story if the toys were ANGRY — imagine if every toy you ever broke or ignored stood up and said 'You didn't take care of me!' That's what happened to the wood people

sections.Story:
The world was QUIET once again.
No people now — just BIRDS and WREN,
Just JAGUAR prowling, DEER at play,
The animals had their OWN day.

The gods said: 'SPEAK! Say our great NAMES!
Praise us like WIND praises FLAMES!'
But animals could only CRY:
SQUAWK and HOWL beneath the sky.

'They cannot PRAISE. They cannot PRAY.
They'll be the FOOD for those who MAY.'
The gods still NEEDED someone who could THINK.
Someone who could stand at CREATION's brink.
sections.Wonder: The animals were beautiful and alive, but they couldn't say the gods' names — so they couldn't be the ones to remember creation. Words and stories keep things ALIVE. What story do YOU want to remember and tell someone else?
sections.Whisper: The assignment of animals as 'food' because they cannot speak sacred names reveals a key Maya concept: the PURPOSE of humans is to be rememberers and praise-givers. Humanity exists to maintain relationship with the divine through LANGUAGE and RITUAL. Without someone who can say the gods' names, creation has no witness. This is not about human superiority — it's about RESPONSIBILITY. Humans are needed because the cosmos requires consciousness to recognize itself.
sections.Original:
— Lewis Spence, Popol Vuh: Mythic and Heroic Sagas of the Kichés —

The Kichés now began to feel the want of fire, and the god Tohil, the creator of fire, supplied them with this element. But soon afterwards a mighty rain extinguished all the fires in the land. Tohil, however, always renewed the supply. And fire in those days was the chief necessity, for as yet there was no sun.

Tulan was a place of misfortune to man, for not only did he suffer from cold and famine, but here his speech was so confounded that the first four men were no longer able to comprehend each other. They determined to leave Tulan, and under the leadership of the god Tohil set out to search for a new abode. On they wandered through innumerable hardships. Many mountains had they to climb, and a long passage to make through the sea which was miraculously divided for their journey from shore to shore. At length they came to a mountain which they called Hacavitz, after one of their gods, and here they rested, for here they had been instructed that they should see the sun. And the sun appeared. Animals and men were transported with delight. All the celestial bodies were now established. But the sun was not as it is to-day.

metadata.maya_source: Popol Vuh, Part 1 — the gods' attempt to have animals praise them. 'They just squawked, they just chattered, they just howled.' Tedlock pp. 66-67
metadata.archetype: The hierarchy of consciousness — beings ranked by their capacity for sacred speech (parallels Hindu Vedic hierarchy, Buddhist six realms, Sufi levels of the soul)
metadata.pop_culture: Like in Brother Bear when Kenai becomes a bear and discovers that animals have their OWN language — just not the one the humans expected

items.name: The Bird Who Called Himself the Sun

sections.Story:
Before the TRUE sun lit the sky,
A MACAW flew outrageously HIGH.
His feathers BLAZED with gold and GREEN,
The brightest bird you've ever SEEN.

SEVEN MACAW! He puffed his CHEST:
'I am the SUN! I am the BEST!
My TEETH are jewels, my EYES are bright,
I light the WORLD with my own LIGHT!'

But he was LYING — just a BIRD
Who wanted everyone's last WORD.
A FAKE sun shining, proud and VAIN,
While the real world waited, dark as RAIN.
sections.Wonder: Seven Macaw PRETENDED to be the sun — he was shiny and loud, but he wasn't really lighting up the world. Have you ever met someone who bragged a lot but wasn't really as great as they said? What's the difference between being BRIGHT and being a real light?
sections.Whisper: Vucub Caquix (Seven Macaw) represents FALSE CONSCIOUSNESS — a being who claims to be the sun and moon before the true sun exists. His jeweled teeth and shining eyes are materialism masquerading as enlightenment. In the Popol Vuh's timeline, this episode occurs in the twilight between failed creations and the Hero Twins' story. Seven Macaw is not evil — he fills a VACUUM. Without a real sun, any shiny thing can claim to be the light.
sections.Original:
— Lewis Spence, Popol Vuh: Mythic and Heroic Sagas of the Kichés —

The language in which the "Popol Vuh" was written, was, as has been said, the Kiché, a dialect of the great Maya-Kiché tongue spoken at the time of the Conquest from the borders of Mexico on the north to those of the present State of Nicaragua on the south; but whereas the Mayan was spoken in Yucatan proper, and the State of Chiapas, the Kiché was the tongue of the peoples of that part of Central America now occupied by the States of Guatemala, Honduras and San Salvador, where it is still used by the natives. It is totally different to the Nahuatl, the language of the people of Anahuac or Mexico, both as regards its origin and structure, and its affinities with other American tongues are even less distinct than the those between the Slavonic and Teutonic groups. Of this tongue the "Popol Vuh" is practically the only monument; at all events the only work by a native of the district in which it was used. A cognate dialect, the Cakchiquel, produced the "Annals" of that people, otherwise known as "The Book of Chilan Balam," a work purely of genealogical interest, which may be consulted in the admirable translation of the late Daniel G.

metadata.maya_source: Popol Vuh, Part 2 — Vucub Caquix's self-glorification. 'I am great. My place is now higher than that of the human work.' Tedlock pp. 73-75
metadata.archetype: The false light — the pretender who claims divine status before the true divine appears (parallels Lucifer, Prometheus, any tyrant claiming to be a god)
metadata.pop_culture: Like Tamatoa the shiny crab in Moana — covered in gold and jewels, bragging about how beautiful he is, but he's just a bully in a sparkly shell

sections.Story:
His TEETH were made of precious STONE,
Turquoise, jade — he LOVED to moan:
'Look at ME! My smile so BRIGHT!
I am the MORNING and the NIGHT!'

He built a THRONE up in the TREES,
Where everyone could bow on KNEES.
His NEST was like a palace GRAND,
The shiniest spot in all the LAND.

Two BOYS watched from the forest FLOOR.
They'd seen this kind of thing BEFORE.
'He's NOT the sun,' the brothers SAID.
'Let's knock that CROWN right off his head.'
sections.Wonder: Seven Macaw put jewels in his teeth to look important. But the two boys watching from below weren't fooled by the sparkle. What helps YOU tell the difference between something that just LOOKS special and something that really IS special?
sections.Whisper: The jeweled teeth are archaeologically accurate: elite Maya did inlay jade, pyrite, and obsidian into their teeth as status markers. The Popol Vuh transforms this practice into a symbol of false authority. When the Hero Twins later remove Seven Macaw's teeth and replace them with white corn kernels, they strip his STATUS and replace it with sustenance — the thing that actually matters. Power vs. nourishment is one of the Popol Vuh's central themes.
sections.Original:
— Lewis Spence, Popol Vuh: Mythic and Heroic Sagas of the Kichés —

After this catastrophe, ere yet the earth was quite recovered from the wrath of the gods, there existed a man "full of pride," whose name was Vukub-Cakix. The name signifies "Seven-times-the-colour-of-fire," or "Very brilliant," and was justified by the fact that its owner's eyes were of silver, his teeth of emerald, and other parts of his anatomy of precious metals. In his own opinion Vukub-Cakix's existence rendered unnecessary that of the sun and the moon, and this egoism so disgusted the gods that they resolved upon his overthrow. His two sons, Zipacna and Cabrakan (earth-heaper [4] (?) and earthquake), were daily employed, the one in heaping up mountains, and the other in demolishing them, and these also incurred the wrath of the immortals. Shortly after the decision of the deities the twin hero-gods Hun-Ahpu and Xbalanque came to earth with the intention of chastising the arrogance of Vukub-Cakix and his progeny.

Now Vukub-Cakix had a great tree of the variety known in Central America as "nanze" or "tapal," bearing a fruit round, yellow, and aromatic, and upon this fruit he depended for his daily sustenance.

metadata.maya_source: Popol Vuh, Part 2 — description of Vucub Caquix's jeweled teeth and shining eyes. Christenson pp. 88-92
metadata.archetype: The vain tyrant — power displayed through ornament rather than service (parallels Narcissus, the Emperor's New Clothes)
metadata.pop_culture: Like Smaug sleeping on his pile of gold in The Hobbit — all that treasure but no one benefits, just a greedy creature admiring himself

sections.Story:
HUNAHPU and XBALANQUE —
Two brothers, quick as MONKEYS play.
They lived with GRANDMA in a HOUSE
As quiet as a little MOUSE.

But these boys weren't ORDINARY kids.
They had a SECRET that their grandma HID.
Their FATHER was a god who DIED,
Playing ball on the UNDERWORLD's dark side.

They picked up BLOWGUNS, strong and LONG,
And aimed at everything that's WRONG.
'That bird's a LIAR,' Hunahpu said.
'Let's teach him TRUTH instead of DREAD.'
sections.Wonder: The Hero Twins were raised by their grandmother and didn't even know their father was a god! Sometimes the most amazing people come from the simplest beginnings. What's something amazing about YOUR family that you're still discovering?
sections.Whisper: The Hero Twins' names encode cosmic meaning: Hunahpu means 'One Blowgunner' (also a day-name in the Maya calendar, associated with the sun), and Xbalanque likely means 'Jaguar Sun' or 'Hidden Sun.' They are raised by their grandmother Xmucane, who is also the creator goddess — one of the divine pair who made the world. The blowgun (pub') was both a real Maya hunting weapon and a mythic instrument. The Twins don't use swords or clubs; they use a weapon that requires BREATH — the same force that spoke the world into being.
sections.Original:
— Lewis Spence, Popol Vuh: Mythic and Heroic Sagas of the Kichés —

Shortly afterwards Xquiq became the mother of twin boys, the heroes of the First Book, Hun-Ahpu, and Xbalanque. These did not find favour in the eyes of Xmucane, their grandmother. Their infantile cries aroused the wrath of this venerable person, and she vented it upon them by turning them out of doors. They speedily took to an outdoor life, however, and became mighty hunters, and expert in the use of their blow-pipes, with which they shot birds and other small game. The ill-treatment which they received from Hunbatz and Hunchouen caused them at last to retaliate, and those who had made their lives miserable were punished by being transformed by the divine children into apes. The venerable Xmucane, filled with grief at the metamorphosis and flight of her ill-starred grandsons, who had made her home joyous with their singing and flute-playing, was told that she would be permitted to behold their faces once more if she could do so without losing her gravity, but their antics and grimaces caused her such merriment that on three separate occasions she was unable to restrain her laughter and the men-monkeys appeared no more.

metadata.maya_source: Popol Vuh, Part 2 — introduction of the Hero Twins and their blowguns. Tedlock pp. 75-79
metadata.archetype: The divine orphans — heroes raised in humble circumstances, unaware of their heritage (parallels Moses, King Arthur, Luke Skywalker, Harry Potter)
metadata.pop_culture: Like Miguel in Coco, raised by his abuela and discovering his real family history was NOTHING like what he'd been told

items.name: The Shot That Broke His Jaw

sections.Story:
Seven Macaw sat in his TREE,
Eating fruit for all to SEE.
Hunahpu AIMED his blowgun HIGH
And shot a dart into the SKY.

CRACK! It hit the macaw's JAW!
The biggest bird you ever SAW
Came TUMBLING, screaming, from his NEST —
But grabbed the boy's ARM from his chest!

He TORE the arm right off, oh NO!
And carried it back to his SHOW.
'I'll ROAST this arm above my FIRE!'
But the twins had tricks that reached much HIGHER.
sections.Wonder: Hunahpu LOST HIS ARM shooting at Seven Macaw! But he didn't give up — he made a plan to get it back. When something goes really wrong in the middle of your plan, do you give up or find a new way?
sections.Whisper: The arm-ripping episode is a signature Maya mythic motif, appearing on Classic Period (250-900 CE) painted ceramics centuries before the Popol Vuh was written down. The severed arm represents SACRIFICE as strategy: Hunahpu gives up part of himself to set up the larger plan. In Maya thought, dismemberment is not defeat — it's TRANSFORMATION. Body parts have their own agency. The arm hung over Seven Macaw's fire becomes the lure that draws him into the Twins' trap.
sections.Original:
— Lewis Spence, Popol Vuh: Mythic and Heroic Sagas of the Kichés —

He had by a wife, Xbakiyalo, two sons, Hunbatz and Hunchouen, men full of wisdom and artistic genius. metadata.maya_source: Popol Vuh, Part 2 — the blowgun attack on Vucub Caquix and the loss of Hunahpu's arm. Tedlock pp. 79-82
metadata.archetype: The wounding of the hero — the price paid in the first encounter with evil (parallels Tyr losing his hand to Fenrir, Horus losing his eye to Set)
metadata.pop_culture: Like in How to Train Your Dragon when Hiccup loses his leg — the hero gives up part of themselves but gains something greater

sections.Story:
Seven Macaw had sons — oh DEAR.
ZIPACNÁ was the one to FEAR.
He lifted MOUNTAINS on his BACK
And set them down with a thunderous CRACK.

'I MADE the mountains!' Zipacná ROARED.
Four hundred BOYS he nearly FLOORED
When they tried to DROP a log on his HEAD.
He let them think that he was DEAD.

But the Twins were SMARTER than brute FORCE.
They made a fake CRAB, of course!
Zipacná LOVED to eat crab MEAT —
He crawled inside and met DEFEAT.
sections.Wonder: Zipacná was SO strong he could carry mountains — but the Twins beat him with a FAKE CRAB, not with fighting. Sometimes the cleverest trick beats the strongest muscle. When have you used your BRAIN instead of your strength to solve a problem?
sections.Whisper: Zipacná (likely meaning 'Mountain-maker') represents RAW GEOLOGICAL FORCE — earthquakes, volcanic uplift. The Four Hundred Boys (Motz) he kills become the Pleiades star cluster, making this a Maya origin story for those stars. The defeat-by-fake-crab is a masterpiece of Maya trickster logic: you don't fight a mountain — you lure it with its appetite. The crab was made of stone, and the mountain that fell on Zipacná turned him to stone. Like defeats like. Christenson notes the crab represents the earth's own creatures turning against false power.
sections.Original:
— Lewis Spence, Popol Vuh: Mythic and Heroic Sagas of the Kichés —

But their mission was not yet complete. The sons of Vukub-Cakix, Zipacna and Cabrakan, remained to be accounted for. Zipacna consented, at the entreaty of four hundred youths, incited by the hero-gods, to assist them in transporting a huge tree which was destined for the roof-tree of a house they were building. Whilst assisting them he was beguiled by them into entering a great ditch which they had dug for the purpose of destroying him, and when once he descended was overwhelmed by tree-trunks by his treacherous acquaintances, who imagined him to be slain. But he took refuge in a side-tunnel of the excavation, cut off his hair and nails for the ants to carry up to his enemies as a sign of his death, waited until the youths had become intoxicated with pulque because of joy at his supposed demise, and then, emerging from the pit, shook the house that the youths had built over his body about their heads, so that all were destroyed in its ruins.

But Hun-Ahpu and Xbalanque were grieved that the four hundred had perished, and laid a more efficacious trap for Zipacna.

metadata.maya_source: Popol Vuh, Part 2 — Zipacná kills the Four Hundred Boys and is defeated by the Twins' stone crab. Tedlock pp. 83-89
metadata.archetype: The earth giant — monstrous strength that must be outwitted, not outfought (parallels Greek Titans, Norse Jötnar, Hindu Asuras)
metadata.pop_culture: Like Maui trapping the sun with a rope in Moana — you can't punch a cosmic force, but you CAN trick it

sections.Story:
CABRACÁN was Seven Macaw's other BOY.
He liked to SHAKE the earth for JOY.
Mountains TREMBLED when he WALKED,
The ground would RUMBLE when he talked.

'I am the EARTHQUAKE!' he would BOAST.
'I can destroy what matters MOST!
One STOMP and every mountain FALLS!'
He knocked down peaks like bowling BALLS.

The Twins said: 'Try THIS bird instead —'
A poisoned TREAT to knock him DEAD.
They roasted bird with SPECIAL clay
And Earthquake CRUMBLED that same day.
sections.Wonder: Earthquake could shake the whole world — but he was beaten by a poisoned bird wrapped in something that LOOKED delicious. Things that look like treats can sometimes be traps. How do you decide if something that looks good is REALLY good for you?
sections.Whisper: Cabracán ('Earthquake') and Zipacná represent two kinds of geological destruction: mountain-BUILDING (Zipacná/volcanism) and mountain-BREAKING (Cabracán/earthquakes). The Twins defeat both with food-based traps — the crab for Zipacná, the poisoned bird for Cabracán. In Maya thought, these are not random monsters but FORCES OF NATURE that must be brought under control before the world is safe for humans. The Popol Vuh is, among other things, a geological origin story told as a bedtime adventure.
sections.Original:
— Lewis Spence, Popol Vuh: Mythic and Heroic Sagas of the Kichés —

But Hun-Ahpu and Xbalanque were grieved that the four hundred had perished, and laid a more efficacious trap for Zipacna. The mountain-bearer, carrying the mountains by night, sought his sustenance by day by the shore of the river, where he lived upon fish and crabs. The hero-gods constructed an artificial crab which they placed in a cavern at the bottom of a deep ravine. The hungry titan descended to the cave, which he entered on all-fours. But a neighbouring mountain had been undermined by the divine brothers, and its bulk was cast upon him. Thus at the foot of Mount Meavan perished the proud "Mountain Maker," whose corpse was turned into stone by the catastrophe.

Of the family of boasters only Cabrakan remained. Discovered by the hero-gods at his favourite pastime of overturning the hills, they enticed him in an easterly direction, challenging him to overthrow a particularly high mountain. On the way they shot a bird with their blow-pipes, and poisoned it with earth. This they gave to Cabrakan to eat. After partaking of the poisoned fare his strength deserted him, and failing to move the mountain he was bound and buried by the victorious hero-gods.

THE SECOND BOOK

metadata.maya_source: Popol Vuh, Part 2 — Cabracán's defeat through poisoned bird coated in earth/plaster. Christenson pp. 100-104
metadata.archetype: The chaos monster tamed — natural destruction brought under cosmic control (parallels Marduk defeating Tiamat, Thor fighting Jörmungandr)
metadata.pop_culture: Like Te Kā in Moana — a force of destruction that seems unstoppable until someone approaches it with the RIGHT offering instead of a weapon

sections.Story:
The Twins found healers — wise and OLD.
(Actually THEMSELVES, in disguise so BOLD!)
'We'll FIX your jaw!' they told the bird.
'We're doctors!' — and he believed each WORD.

They pulled his JEWELED teeth right OUT
And put in corn instead — no CLOUT.
They dimmed his SHINING eyes to GREY.
The false sun FADED, dark as clay.

Seven Macaw, no longer BRIGHT,
Lost all his POWER, lost his MIGHT.
The FAKE sun set. The sky went DIM.
And the world stopped believing HIM.
sections.Wonder: The Twins dressed up as OLD healers to trick Seven Macaw — they took out his jewel-teeth and put in plain corn instead. His sparkle was GONE. Real light doesn't come from jewels — it comes from inside. What makes YOU shine that has nothing to do with how you look?
sections.Whisper: The Twins' disguise as elderly healers (aj kununel — 'grandparent-doctors') is the first of many SHAPESHIFTING tricks. Replacing jeweled teeth with white corn kernels is a profound symbol: corn is LIFE, jade is STATUS. The false sun loses his glitter and is left with sustenance instead of glamour. Seven Macaw and his sons represent the world BEFORE cosmic order — a world of brute force and vanity. Their defeat clears the stage for the real story: the ballgame between life and death.
sections.Original:
— Lewis Spence, Popol Vuh: Mythic and Heroic Sagas of the Kichés —

Now Vukub-Cakix had a great tree of the variety known in Central America as "nanze" or "tapal," bearing a fruit round, yellow, and aromatic, and upon this fruit he depended for his daily sustenance. One day on going to partake of it for his morning meal he mounted to its summit in order to espy the choicest fruits, when to his great indignation he discovered that Hun-Ahpu and Xbalanque had been before him, and had almost denuded the tree of its produce. The hero-gods, who lay concealed within the foliage, now added injury to theft by hurling at Vukub-Cakix a dart from a blow-pipe, which had the effect of precipitating him from the summit of the tree to the earth. He arose in great wrath, bleeding profusely from a severe wound in the jaw. Hun-Ahpu then threw himself upon Vukub-Cakix, who in terrible anger seized the god by the arm and wrenched it from the body. He then proceeded to his dwelling, where he was met and anxiously interrogated by his spouse Chimalmat.

metadata.maya_source: Popol Vuh, Part 2 — the Twins disguised as healers, removing Vucub Caquix's teeth and eyes. Tedlock pp. 82-83
metadata.archetype: The trickster healer — defeating evil by pretending to cure it (parallels Odysseus's disguises, Br'er Rabbit, Anansi stories)
metadata.pop_culture: Like the Wizard of Oz being revealed as just a man behind a curtain — all that POWER was just a show, and it takes a child to see through it

sections.Story:
ONE HUNAHPU and SEVEN HUNAHPU too —
Two brothers who knew what they loved to DO.
They played the BALLGAME every single DAY,
On a court of stone where the mountains PLAY.

The RUBBER ball bounced hard and FAST.
They hit it with their HIPS — a mighty BLAST!
No hands, no feet — just BODY strong,
Bouncing that ball the whole day LONG.

The SOUND of bouncing echoed DEEP,
Down through the EARTH where dark things SLEEP.
Down to XIBALBA — the place BELOW,
Where the lords of DEATH put on their show.
sections.Wonder: The first ballplayers loved their game so much that the SOUND of their ball bounced all the way down to the underworld! When you love doing something so much that you do it every single day, what is it?
sections.Whisper: The Maya ballgame (pitz) was not a sport — it was a COSMIC RITUAL. The rubber ball represented the sun's movement; the court represented the threshold between life and death. Archaeological evidence shows ballcourts at nearly every major Maya site. One Hunahpu and Seven Hunahpu are the FIRST GENERATION of divine ballplayers, and their game literally disturbs the underworld. The Popol Vuh makes clear: playing the game is not optional — it is how the living communicate with the dead.

metadata.maya_source: Popol Vuh, Part 2 — One Hunahpu and Seven Hunahpu as the first ballplayers. Tedlock pp. 95-97
metadata.archetype: The divine game — reality as cosmic play (parallels Hindu lila, Greek agon, Chinese wei-qi as cosmic metaphor)
metadata.pop_culture: Like the opening of The Road to El Dorado — two friends whose favorite game is about to lead them into an adventure bigger than they ever imagined

items.name: The Ball That Woke the Dead

sections.Story:
BOUNCE! BOUNCE! BOUNCE! upon the COURT.
The brothers played their favorite SPORT.
But every THUMP went underground,
A RUMBLING, tumbling, thumping SOUND.

Down in XIBALBA, dark and DIM,
The DEATH LORDS stirred on every LIMB.
One Death and Seven DEATH woke up —
'WHO is bouncing on our ROOF? SHUT UP!'

They sent a MESSAGE flying HIGH:
'Come DOWN and play — or say GOODBYE.
We CHALLENGE you to play OUR game,
In OUR court — where nothing's quite the SAME.'
sections.Wonder: Imagine bouncing a ball on the ground and waking up DEATH itself! The Death Lords were annoyed — like grumpy downstairs neighbors. Sometimes doing what you love disturbs others. How do you keep doing what you love while respecting those around you?
sections.Whisper: The Death Lords' names are extraordinary: One Death (Jun Kame) and Seven Death (Wuqub' Kame) are the rulers, but their court includes: Scab Stripper, Blood Gatherer, Wing, Packstrap, Bone Staff, Skull Staff, Pus Master, and Jaundice Master. Each name represents a specific DISEASE or CAUSE OF DEATH. Xibalba is not 'hell' — it is the natural world of death, sickness, and decomposition. The lords are not evil; they are the MECHANISMS of mortality, personified.
sections.Original:
— Lewis Spence, Popol Vuh: Mythic and Heroic Sagas of the Kichés —

THE HARRYING OF XIBALBA

The doings of Hun-Ahpu and Xbalanque, in Xibalba, may be regarded either as the Kiché account of the adventures of two veritable heroes in a new land, or as the visitation of divine beings to Hades for the express purpose of conquering death. But by the period of the formation of the myth it is probable that Xibalba had become confounded with the Place of the Dead, and was regarded as a fit theatre for the prodigies of craft and valour of the young hero-gods. The Kiché Hades had, in fact, evolved from the old northern home, exactly as had the Mexican Mictlan, which, although a subterranean locality, was also, and separately, a northern country. A complete Place of the Dead had been established, and the gods, to show their contempt of death, must descend thereto and emerge triumphant. The idea of metempsychosis was known to the American aboriginal mind. "We Indians shall not for ever die; even the grains of corn we put under the earth grow up and become living things," is the noble and touching reply of a chief to the interrogation of a Moravian Brother, regarding the native belief in immortality.

metadata.maya_source: Popol Vuh, Part 2 — the sound of the ball disturbing the lords of Xibalba. 'What's happening on the face of the earth? They are just stomping and shouting.' Tedlock pp. 97-98
metadata.archetype: The summons to the underworld — the call that the hero cannot refuse (parallels Orpheus called to Hades, Inanna's descent, Odysseus's nekuia)
metadata.pop_culture: Like in Coco when Miguel accidentally ends up in the Land of the Dead — the boundary between worlds is thinner than you think, and sometimes you cross it by accident

sections.Story:
Four OWLS flew up from underground —
Messenger birds without a SOUND.
They carried words from death's dark THRONE:
'Come PLAY with us. Don't come ALONE.'

The brothers READ the summons twice.
The words were COLD as winter ice.
'We must GO,' said One Hunahpu BRAVE.
'A challenge is a challenge — we can't just WAVE.'

'But bring your GEAR,' the owls hooted LOW.
'Your rubber BALL, your yokes — let's GO.'
The brothers left their GEAR above,
Tucked safely in the RAFTERS of their home they love.
sections.Wonder: The brothers received a scary invitation from the underworld — but they felt they HAD to go because a challenge is a challenge. Some invitations are hard to say no to. How do you know the difference between being BRAVE and being RECKLESS?
sections.Whisper: The four messenger owls (Chabi Tucur, Huracán Tucur, Caquix Tucur, Holom Tucur) are specific species of Mesoamerican owls, each associated with death omens. In Maya iconography, owls are psychopomps — guides between worlds. The brothers hide their ballgame equipment in the RAFTERS of their house — this detail becomes crucial, because the Hero Twins later discover it there. Every detail in the Popol Vuh is a seed planted for later harvest. The rafters = the space between earth and sky, where sacred things wait.

— Lewis Spence, Popol Vuh: Mythic and Heroic Sagas of the Kichés —

This reached the ears of the monarchs of that place, Hun-Came and Vukub-Came, who, after consulting their counsellors, challenged the strangers to a game of ball, with the object of defeating and disgracing them.

For this purpose they dispatched four messengers in the shape of owls.

metadata.maya_source: Popol Vuh, Part 2 — the owl messengers and the brothers' departure. Christenson pp. 112-116
metadata.archetype: The threshold messenger — the herald who announces the descent (parallels Hermes as psychopomp, Valkyries, the Angel of Death)
metadata.pop_culture: Like the Hogwarts letter in Harry Potter — a message arrives that changes everything, and once you read it, you can't pretend the other world doesn't exist

sections.Story:
Down, DOWN they went through caverns DEEP,
Down STAIRS so steep they couldn't SLEEP.
They crossed a RIVER made of PUSS,
Another one of BLOOD — no fuss.

A river made of SCORPIONS — YIKES!
And pointed rocks like iron SPIKES.
At every CROSSROADS they went WRONG,
The path to death was dark and LONG.

They reached a place where ROADS divide:
Black road, white road, RED and wide.
'This WAY,' the black road seemed to CALL.
And the brothers walked right into XIBALBA's hall.
sections.Wonder: The road to Xibalba had rivers of scorpions and blood — and FOUR different colored roads to choose from! The brothers picked the WRONG one. When you come to a fork in the road (in a story or in real life), how do you decide which way to go?
sections.Whisper: The road to Xibalba includes rivers of blood, puss, and scorpions — and then a CROSSROADS of four colored roads (black, white, red, yellow). These correspond to the four cardinal directions in Maya cosmology (each associated with a color, a tree, a bird, and a cosmic force). The brothers choose the Black Road (west, the direction of death and the setting sun). The crossroads is a COSMIC MAP — choosing the wrong path isn't random; it reveals what you're drawn to. The Hero Twins, when they later face this same crossroads, make a DIFFERENT choice.

— Lewis Spence, Popol Vuh: Mythic and Heroic Sagas of the Kichés —

For this purpose they dispatched four messengers in the shape of owls.

metadata.maya_source: Popol Vuh, Part 2 — the descent and the crossroads of four roads. Tedlock pp. 99-102
metadata.archetype: The descent to the underworld — the katabasis (parallels Orpheus, Inanna, Odysseus, Dante, Persephone)
metadata.pop_culture: Like the Mines of Moria in Lord of the Rings — going underground, facing horrors at every turn, and realizing you might not come back the same way

sections.Story:
The DEATH LORDS laughed: 'Welcome, FRIENDS!
Here in Xibalba, the FUN never ends!
First test: DARK HOUSE — here's a TORCH.
Keep it BURNING on this porch.'

But the torch was NOT to be BURNED through!
'Return it WHOLE — that's what you DO.
If it's GONE by morning light,
You LOSE the game. Goodnight, GOODNIGHT.'

The brothers LIT the torch — oh NO.
They watched it BURN down, soft and SLOW.
By morning it was ASHES, black.
They'd failed the test. No going BACK.
sections.Wonder: The Death Lords gave the brothers a torch and said 'Keep it lit all night — but bring it back WHOLE in the morning.' That's an impossible trick! The brothers fell for it. Some tests aren't fair — they're DESIGNED so you can't win. What do you do when the rules are rigged?
sections.Whisper: Dark House (Quequma Ha) is the first of Xibalba's SIX test houses. Each is a trap designed to be failed: keep a torch burning but return it unused. This is the Popol Vuh's profound insight — the lords of death don't fight fair. They present PARADOXES: tasks that contradict themselves. The first generation of heroes (the Fathers) fail because they take the test at face value. The Hero Twins later solve this same test by using fireflies instead of fire — they REFRAME the problem rather than solving it as stated.
sections.Original:
— Lewis Spence, Popol Vuh: Mythic and Heroic Sagas of the Kichés —

It is evident that the divine brothers feared ridicule, and profiting from the disasters of their father and uncle made sure of knowing the names of the chief Xibalbans ere they set out. In like manner they avoided making an obeisance to the dummy figures to which their predecessors had bowed so profoundly. The American savage, grave and reserved, cannot abide ridicule. He shrinks from it in a manner which a less self-regarding or a more self-assured people cannot comprehend. The other tests--the "House of Tigers," and the "House of Cold," and the various torments mentioned in the Second Book are much what might be expected from a barbarian idea of death--no more horrible, perhaps, than the European idea of Hell in the Middle Ages, certainly not more fear-compelling than the picture of Dante.

The American peoples are at one in their belief in a Paradise, a Place of Joy, if not of Reward. Their Hades appears to have been reserved almost entirely for the unillustrious.

metadata.maya_source: Popol Vuh, Part 2 — Dark House, the first of Xibalba's test houses. Christenson pp. 120-124
metadata.archetype: The impossible test — the trial designed to be failed (parallels Psyche's tasks from Aphrodite, Rumpelstiltskin, the Sphinx's riddle)
metadata.pop_culture: Like the Kobayashi Maru test in Star Trek — the no-win scenario that Captain Kirk beat by changing the RULES instead of playing by them

sections.Story:
They LOST the ballgame, dark and SORE.
The Death Lords won — they wanted MORE.
They took the brothers' LIVES that night,
Snuffed them out like CANDLE light.

One Hunahpu's HEAD they cut off CLEAN
And hung it up where it could be SEEN —
In a CALABASH tree, round and DRY,
A skull among the fruit, hung HIGH.

The Death Lords LAUGHED: 'Let this be a SIGN!
No one PLAYS in courts of MINE!'
But they didn't know what SKULLS can do
When the right young WOMAN wanders through.
sections.Wonder: The fathers LOST and their heads were hung in a tree. That sounds like the end of the story — but it's actually the BEGINNING. Sometimes the worst thing that happens plants the seed for something amazing. Can you think of a time when something sad led to something wonderful?
sections.Whisper: The sacrifice of One Hunahpu is the HINGE of the entire Popol Vuh. His head, placed in a calabash tree, transforms the barren tree into a fruit-bearing one — the skull IS the fruit. This image appears on countless Maya painted vessels: the severed head as seed, death as the prerequisite for new life. The lords of Xibalba forbid anyone from approaching the tree, which of course is exactly what draws Xquic (Blood Moon maiden) to it. In Maya agricultural theology, the seed must be BURIED (killed) for corn to grow.

— Lewis Spence, Popol Vuh: Mythic and Heroic Sagas of the Kichés —
